summary: Reflecting on five years of working on Ruby on Rails ahead of RailsConf '08, DHH expresses genuine surprise and satisfaction that his passion for the project hasn't faded. He argues that sustained commitment driven by love of the work itself produces greater rewards than external incentives. Despite Rails having already 'won' by many measures—industry impact, adoption, conferences—he finds that what truly matters is the ongoing excitement about the work. He closes with advice to find a vocation where you enjoy the act itself, not just the outcomes.
key_points: Five years into Rails development, DHH's enthusiasm remains undiminished, proving it wasn't just a phase or a run toward an exit event
Intrinsic motivation—doing what you love for its own sake—produces greater rewards than external incentives
Rails has already achieved significant industry impact, but those external markers of success matter less than the ongoing joy of the work
Long-term commitment to a project is more likely when contributors genuinely love the work itself
money_quotes: It didn't pass, it wasn't just a phase, it wasn't a run for an exit event.
When you do what you love for the sake of itself, the rewards are so much greater than if you just do it for external incentives.
For lots of measures of "winning", we've long since won with Ruby on Rails.
What matters is getting excited about continuing the work.
There's not enough time to spend it doing anything else.
key_insight: Lasting commitment to a project comes from loving the work itself, not from chasing external rewards or exit events.
